data.content:
Now I have enough information to give a comprehensive answer. Here's what changed in .NET 10 for each of your scenarios:

---

## 1. NU1510 warnings ŌĆö "pruned" PackageReferences

**What changed:** .NET 10 enables **package pruning** (`PrunePackageReference`) by default for projects targeting `net10.0`. Packages already provided by the .NET runtime (e.g., `System.Text.Json`) are automatically pruned from the dependency graph.

For **direct** PackageReferences that match a pruned package, NuGet applies `PrivateAssets="all"` and `IncludeAssets="none"` implicitly and raises **NU1510** telling you the reference is unnecessary.

**Fix:** Remove the redundant `<PackageReference>`. If you still need it for other TFMs (e.g., `net48`), condition it:
```xml
<PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" Version="9.0.4"
                  Condition="'$(TargetFramework)' == 'net48'" />
```
To disable pruning entirely:
```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <RestoreEnablePackagePruning>false</RestoreEnablePackagePruning>
</PropertyGroup>
```

---

## 2. Analyzer-only package missing from deps.json

**What changed:** Package pruning removes framework-provided packages from the deps.json output. If your analyzer package happens to share a package ID that the SDK recognizes as "supplied by the platform", pruning applies `IncludeAssets="none"` and it vanishes from deps.json.

**Fix:** Either exclude the package from pruning, or update your deployment script to not rely on deps.json for analyzer-only packages (they have no runtime assets anyway). You can also set `RestoreEnablePackagePruning=false`.

---

## 3. PrunePackageReference + direct references in .NET 10

In .NET 9, pruning was opt-in and only affected **transitive** packages. In .NET 10, pruning is **on by default** and also affects **direct** PackageReferences:

| Dependency type | .NET 9 (opt-in) | .NET 10 (default) |
|---|---|---|
| Transitive | Removed from graph | Removed from graph |
| Direct | Not affected | `PrivateAssets=all` + `IncludeAssets=none` applied; **NU1510** raised if fully removable |
| ProjectReference | Not pruned | Not pruned (but **NU1511** raised if ID matches) |

---

## 4. `dotnet new sln` now produces `.slnx` (XML format)

**What changed:** Starting with .NET 10, `dotnet new sln` defaults to the new **`.slnx` XML-based solution format** instead of the classic `.sln` text format. The `.slnx` file has no project GUIDs, so scripts that parse GUIDs from `.sln` files will break.

**Fix:** Use `--format sln` to get the classic format:
```bash
dotnet new sln --name MySolution --format sln
```

---

## 5. Satellite assemblies for custom cultures (e.g., "x-custom")

**What changed:** NuGet 6.13 (shipped with .NET 9.0.200 SDK) expanded the locale parser to support **three-character language codes** (fixing [NuGet#12253](https://github.com/NuGet/Home/issues/12253)). In .NET 10, the SDK's satellite assembly handling has stricter culture validation. Custom/non-standard culture names like `x-custom` may no longer be recognized as valid satellite assembly cultures, causing them to be excluded from the build output.

**Fix:** Register your custom culture using `CultureInfo.RegisterCustomCulture()` or ensure your culture name conforms to BCP-47 standards. Alternatively, you may need to explicitly include the assembly using MSBuild items rather than relying on automatic satellite assembly detection.

---

## 6. PackageReference without Version attribute fails

**What changed:** In .NET 10/NuGet 7.0, a `<PackageReference>` without a `Version` attribute now **errors** unless you are using **Central Package Management (CPM)** with a `Directory.Packages.props` that defines a corresponding `<PackageVersion>`.

"Implicit resolution from a parent" was never an officially supported feature ŌĆö transitive version resolution doesn't apply to direct references. NuGet 7.0 enforces this more strictly.

**Fix:** Either:
- Add an explicit `Version` attribute:
  ```xml
  <PackageReference Include="MyPackage" Version="1.2.3" />
  ```
- Adopt [Central Package Management](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/nuget/consume-packages/central-package-management) with `Directory.Packages.props`.

---

## 7. `NUGET_ENABLE_ENHANCED_HTTP_RETRY` + HTTP sources + `dotnet package list`

**What changed:** NuGet 7.0 (shipped with .NET 10) now **errors when a non-HTTPS resource is used** within a service index ([NuGet#13364](https://github.com/NuGet/Home/issues/13364)). This means HTTP (non-TLS) NuGet sources may produce errors rather than just warnings. The `allowInsecureConnections="true"` attribute on the source in `nuget.config` is required for HTTP sources.

Additionally, the new noun-first command form `dotnet package list` is an alias for `dotnet list package` ŌĆö both work, but note that HTTP source restrictions apply regardless.

**Fix:** Migrate your NuGet sources to HTTPS. If you must use HTTP, explicitly opt in:
```xml
<packageSources>
  <add key="myHttpSource" value="http://my-server/nuget"
       allowInsecureConnections="true" />
</packageSources>
```
